coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical-thermal modeling of phase change material battery thermal management systems: investigating mesh types for accurate simulations

coredata.dc:description: Computational techniques have been extensively used in the analysis of heat transfer within battery thermal management systems (BTMS). A fundamental and critical initial step in any numerical analysis is the meshing process, which involves subdividing the geometry into numerous small control volumes, or elements. Here, we investigated the accuracy of the simulated thermal performance of a BTMS using phase change material (PCM) with three different mesh types having: hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral elements. A detailed electrochemical-thermal model is used for modeling heat generation within a lithium-ion battery. In this model, a pseudo two-dimensional model captures the internal dynamics of the battery and then is integrated with a three-dimensional conjugate heat transfer model. Furthermore, the enthalpy-porosity method is employed for PCM simulation using computational fluid dynamics. Among the three mesh types, the hexahedral mesh demonstrated the closest agreement with experimental data, yielding smooth temperature gradients and PCM liquid fraction contours in post-processing. The polyhedral mesh, while slightly less accurate than the hexahedral mesh, provided a computational advantage, requiring only about a fifth of the elements compared to the hexahedral mesh and a quarter compared to the tetrahedral mesh. This computational efficiency makes the polyhedral mesh the most economical in terms of computational resources. However, tetrahedral mesh, though better suited for complex geometries, exhibited the highest computational cost and produced the least accurate results, making it less favorable for PCM-based BTMS simulations. To further improve the trade-off between computational cost and accuracy, a hybrid mesh configuration is introduced, combining polyhedral and hexahedral elements to enhance simulation efficiency while preserving accuracy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Computational techniques have been extensively used in the analysis of heat transfer within battery thermal management systems (BTMS). A fundamental and critical initial step in any numerical analysis is the meshing process, which involves subdividing the geometry into numerous small control volumes, or elements. Here, we investigated the accuracy of the simulated thermal performance of a BTMS using phase change material (PCM) with three different mesh types having: hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral elements. A detailed electrochemical-thermal model is used for modeling heat generation within a lithium-ion battery. In this model, a pseudo two-dimensional model captures the internal dynamics of the battery and then is integrated with a three-dimensional conjugate heat transfer model. Furthermore, the enthalpy-porosity method is employed for PCM simulation using computational fluid dynamics. Among the three mesh types, the hexahedral mesh demonstrated the closest agreement with experimental data, yielding smooth temperature gradients and PCM liquid fraction contours in post-processing. The polyhedral mesh, while slightly less accurate than the hexahedral mesh, provided a computational advantage, requiring only about a fifth of the elements compared to the hexahedral mesh and a quarter compared to the tetrahedral mesh. This computational efficiency makes the polyhedral mesh the most economical in terms of computational resources. However, tetrahedral mesh, though better suited for complex geometries, exhibited the highest computational cost and produced the least accurate results, making it less favorable for PCM-based BTMS simulations. To further improve the trade-off between computational cost and accuracy, a hybrid mesh configuration is introduced, combining polyhedral and hexahedral elements to enhance simulation efficiency while preserving accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing demand for energy, and rising fossil fuels consumption, present major concerns related to global climate change. The health and environmental impacts of pollutants and greenhouse gases from the transportation sector are substantial. These challenges highlight the urgent need for the advancement of renewable energy sources. To curb pollutant emissions and achieve emissions targets, the automobile industry in many countries is increasingly adopting electric vehicles (EVs) [1,2].

Among the various energy storage options for EVs, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are more favorable because of their properties such as long cycle life and low self-discharge rate [3]. This matter of enhancing the cycle life of batteries has become an important subject in many studies [4,5]. Due to the safety and high-energy density requirements of LIBs [6], a battery thermal management system (BTMS) is needed to assist in controlling the LIB temperature within the ideal operating range of 15 °C to 40 °C [7,8]. Implementation of an efficient BTMS within an EV helps to improve performance and durability of batteries [9]. Therefore, optimal thermal management is necessary to uphold battery performance, energy storage, and charging capability, as excessive heat can compromise these critical functions [10]. Various methods such as liquid cooling [11–13], air cooling [14], direct contact immersion cooling [15,16], heat pipe cooling [17], and phase change material (PCM) cooling [18] have been utilized in BTMSs to control thermal behavior and ensure high efficiency and battery health. Through efficient management of the battery temperature, an improved BTMS can increase the driving range, overall performance, and safety of the passengers [19,20]. In contrast to conventional thermal management procedures, PCMs represent an energy-efficient method to maintain temperature and help to eliminate cooling system complexity [21].

PCMs are materials that store thermal energy by utilizing their latent heat of phase transformation at a relatively constant temperature [22,23]. They absorb and release a considerable amount of heat through solid-to-liquid and liquid-to-solid phase transitions, respectively [24–26]. In PCM-based BTMSs, the PCM maintains the battery temperature at a safe working level [27]. PCMs effectively regulate battery temperature without continuous energy consumption. This passive cooling method reduces the reliance on external mechanical or electrical cooling systems, which often require additional components, complexity, and energy input. Utilizing PCM in a battery enhances safety, prolongs battery lifespan, and improves overall efficiency in various applications, including EVs [28]. Ren et al. [29] examined the influence of using single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) dispersed into three different types of PCM to regulate the LIB temperature through a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) approach. Numerical findings demonstrated that the nano-enhanced phase change materials significantly enhance cooling performance of LIBs due to the superior convection effects in the liquid phase. Tian et al. [30] prepared two distinct types of composite PCM, having high and low effective thermal conductivity, to find the appropriate PCM for controlling the LIB performance in a battery pack at low ambient temperatures. They concluded that a paraffin wax/expanded graphite composite PCM (PCM with a high effective thermal conductivity) is more effective in enhancing the battery packs performance at low ambient temperatures.

Besides the pure PCMs, hybrid PCMs have also been used in BTMSs. Thawkar et al. [31] determined that a heat-pipe-PCM hybrid BTMS performs significantly better than other BTMSs. Najafi et al. [32] studied thermal uniformity of a hybrid BTMS using metal foam, PCM, and counterflow minichannels. Their analysis revealed that the hybrid system could lower the maximum battery temperature by up to 11.5 °C while maintaining a uniform temperature distribution. Moreover, the hybrid BTMS kept the battery's peak temperature within the ideal range, even under fast discharging, extreme environmental conditions, and a real drive cycle.

Numerous studies have employed CFD methods to analyze and optimize heat dissipation and temperature regulation in batteries. CFD has an important role to play in technological development. Engineers and scientists routinely employ commercial CFD software or develop custom codes to simulate unique effects [33]. CFD has applications in various research fields such as modeling fluid flows and heat transfer [34–38]. Furthermore, CFD is required in scientific visualization for accurate analysis which is typically not available with experimental methods. The continuous advancement of CFD methods not only helps engineers to improve the design of industrial processes but also contributes to the future of scientific research and technological innovation [39]. Among various applications, CFD serves as a tool to analyze the thermal performance of BTMS in EVs. Carello et al. [40] studied thermal challenges within BTMS using CFD methods. They evaluated and compared indirect liquid cold plates with direct immersion liquid cooling.

Different mesh types are routinely used in CFD modeling. Each mesh type can be used in various applications with varying computational time and accuracy. Depending on the complexity of the geometry and the specific simulation requirements, either structured or unstructured meshes are employed [41]. Table 1 presents a list of previous numerical research studies that use various cooling methods and different types of mesh elements to conduct CFD simulation studies.

The selection of mesh type and density influences the accuracy and computational efficiency of BTMS CFD simulations. Various mesh types, including hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral have been utilized in different studies [45–47], demonstrating that the mesh type can significantly affect the numerical accuracy of simulations and computational performance. For instance, employing a finer mesh with a higher number of elements may not always lead to a significant improvement in accuracy of the results, but can enhance numerical resolution and minimize discretization errors if supported by adequate computational resources [51,52]. Moreover, the selection of mesh type can affect the overall simulation results, which requires careful consideration before further analysis. Comparison of different mesh types, such as unstructured and structured meshes, has shown varying effects on simulation outcomes. Previous work has emphasized the importance of grid-independence studies and appropriate mesh type selection for better simulation results [53].

To the best of the authors' knowledge, although many studies have used hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral mesh elements, no research has compared the accuracy and computational cost of these three mesh types for PCM-based BTMS simulation. This study aims to find the most suitable mesh element when simulating the PCM melting process for BTMS applications. Three types of meshes, including structured meshes with hexahedral elements, unstructured meshes with tetrahedral elements, and unstructured meshes with polyhedral elements, are used in the current study. Parameters such as deviation from experimental results, mesh quality metrics, computational cost (including number of cells and iterations), and accuracy of the results during postprocessing, have been analyzed. Furthermore, a detailed electrochemical-thermal model is employed for modeling heat generation of the nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) LIB cell (18650 NMC 811). Finally, a hybrid mesh configuration is suggested as a promising approach to balance simulation accuracy and efficiency. In summary, the results of this research develop CFD modeling guidelines for PCM-based BTMSs and provide practical insights that engineers and researchers can directly apply in their CFD simulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology used for conducting CFD simulations
Phase change material model in a cylindrical enclosure
ce:section.ce:para: Phase change process is simulated via the enthalpy-porosity method by employing a same problem addressed in Ref. [54], as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). In this CFD simulation, the n-eicosane PCM is utilized within a three-dimensional enclosure (with cylindrical shape). As demonstrated in Fig. 1 (b), the upper side of the enclosure is insulated, while the remaining sides are subjected to constant temperature boundary conditions. Additionally, an acrylic plate is placed beneath the cylindrical enclosure, with insulated boundary conditions applied to the side walls. The insulated boundary condition is applied by considering zero heat flux over the surface. Moreover, the interfaces between the two distinct zones are modeled using a thermally coupled wall condition.

To study the effect of different mesh types on accuracy of the BTMS simulation, a PCM-based BTMS is modeled. In this BTMS, a cylindrical LIB (18650 NMC 811) is encased in pure n-eicosane PCM and enclosed within an Al enclosure, as demonstrated in Fig. 2 . Natural convection heat transfer is considered on the outside of Al enclosure walls and top side of the system, while the bottom side is thermally insulated. To incorporate natural convection effects, an averaged heat transfer coefficient of 5.7 W/(m2ˑK) is used, as determined from previous experimental findings in Ref. [55]. In the solver, the insulated and natural convection boundary conditions are implemented by applying zero heat flux and specifying a convective heat transfer coefficient along with the free stream temperature, respectively. The free stream temperature is assumed to be 27 °C. Moreover, the initial temperature is 32 °C for the entire BTMS, and the results are calculated for a 3C discharge rate.

In the current study, n-eicosane PCM is utilized because of its affordability, stability, and appropriate melting point. Table 2 demonstrates the thermophysical properties of all utilized materials in this study. To avoid issues caused by the discontinuity of thermophysical properties within the mushy zone during CFD simulations, a user defined function is developed and employed to linearly adjust these properties according to the temperature in this region. The PCM viscosity (
μ

To model the melting process of PCM in numerical simulation, the enthalpy-porosity method is employed. In this method, the principle of equalizing liquid fraction and porosity is used to provide accurate results [60]. Moreover, several assumptions have been considered in the present study to simplify the CFD simulations, as follows:
•
The melted PCM is modeled as Newtonian and incompressible.
•
Any volumetric changes during the melting of PCM are ignored.
•
Boussinesq approximation is used to consider buoyancy forces in the natural convection within PCM.
•
The energy equation does not account for radiation and viscous dissipation effects.
•
Thermal resistance at solid contact surfaces is disregarded.

A comprehensive electrochemical-thermal model, which considers both electrochemical and heat transfer reactions, is used for an NMC 811 cell. The approach employs a widely used multiphysics framework in battery research, incorporating a pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) model for capturing the internal dynamics of an LIB, and is later integrated into a three-dimensional conjugate heat transfer (CHT) model. While this study focuses on an NMC 811 cell and n-eicosane PCM, the methodology and findings regarding mesh performance are broadly applicable, as the numerical behavior of different mesh types is primarily governed by heat transfer mechanisms and phase change processes rather than specific material properties.

A schematic representation of the electrochemical-thermal model coupled with CHT model is illustrated in Fig. 3 . The left-to-right arrow indicates the transfer of total heat generation data from the electrochemical model to the CHT model, while the right-to-left arrow represents the feedback of volume-averaged temperature data from the thermal model to the electrochemical model which works as a fully coupled electrochemical thermal numerical framework.

The initial temperature for the battery and all other components is set to 23 °C for validation of the model. Under this methodology, the governing equations account for electrochemical kinetics, as well as energy, mass, and charge, alongside CHT. The P2D model consists of three main components: a graphite-based negative electrode (LiyC6), an anode, a LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC 811) positive electrode, a cathode, and a separator. The electrolyte includes LiPF6 dissolved in ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate in a nonaqueous solution. The P2D method used in this study captures the movement of species and charges inside the electrodes, including both the electrolyte and solid phases.

Table 4 summarizes the detailed electrochemical equation with corresponding boundary conditions used to model the discharge process. Table 5 summarizes the heat generation process. All associated parameters used to develop these electrochemical reactions are a dependent combination of works in Refs. [61–64].

Moreover, the energy equation for LIB is as follows:

The boundary conditions, which are explained in Table 4, are applied using the solver. These include lithium diffusion in the solid phase, which follows Fick's second law at the surface. The lithium flux is determined by the local current density, driving intercalation or deintercalation and the boundary condition which is shown in Eq. (12). In the electrolyte phase, lithium-ion (Li+) transport is governed by both diffusion and migration, directing ion flow toward the cathode during discharge. A no-flux boundary condition is imposed at the current collectors as shown in Eqs. (13) to (15), while the conservation of mass of Li atoms in the solid at the surface and Li+ in the electrolyte are considered by Eqs. (16) and (17). The electrochemical reaction rate equation defines a fundamental relationship between the active material in spherical coordinates and the electrolyte, representing the interaction of electrode potential, current density, and local species concentration at the electrode-electrolyte interface. This relationship is mathematically characterized by the Butler-Volmer equation, which describes charge transfer kinetics as shown in Eqs. (19) and (20).

The finite volume method is used to simulate the PCM melting process and heat transfer within a BTMS. ANSYS Fluent is utilized for solving all governing equations. The double-precision solver is chosen for the numerical simulation to enhance accuracy and convergence, addressing the high aspect ratio of certain mesh elements. For calculation, the coupled algorithm is used to compute all equations simultaneously. The pressure equation is discretized by the PRESTO method, while the second-order upwind scheme is applied to the other equations. To improve the convergence rate of computations, under-relaxation factors for pressure, momentum, density, liquid fraction, and energy are set at 0.3, 0.5, 1, 0.9, and 0.9, correspondingly. Up to 300 iterations per time step are specified in addition to setting the convergence criteria of 10-3, 10-5, and 10-8 for the continuity, momentum, and energy equations, respectively. All CFD simulations are performed on a workstation powered by an Intel Xeon E3-1240 V6 Processor and 16 GB of RAM. Moreover, parallel processing with four physical cores has been used for numerical simulations.

ANSYS ICEM CFD software is used to produce meshes in the present study. The Blocking concept and Delaunay mesh methods are utilized to develop the structured mesh with hexahedral elements and the unstructured mesh composed of tetrahedral elements, respectively. The polyhedral unstructured mesh is generated by converting tetrahedral elements to polyhedral elements using the ANSYS Fluent Meshing tools. During this process, the quality of the polyhedral mesh is maintained at the same level as the tetrahedral mesh.

In general, raising the number of mesh elements enhances the accuracy of calculation results; however, it also increases the computational time. Conversely, reducing the number of mesh elements reduces computational time at the expense of solution accuracy. To balance time and accuracy, a mesh independence analysis is conducted for all three different mesh types in Fig. 4 . This analysis focuses on the PCM liquid fraction variation—a key indicator of PCM-based BTMS performance—during the PCM melting process within a cylindrical enclosure. Additionally, a time step independence analysis is performed in Fig. 5 to evaluate the impact of larger time steps on computational cost and accuracy. To obtain a reasonable comparison between all three mesh types used in this study, an equivalent element size (
d

) is defined and is shown in Eq. (25), which can be described as the cube root of the average volume of the elements used in the simulation. This equivalent size provides an approximate unique size of individual elements for hexahedral and tetrahedral meshes. Table 6 outlines the details of the investigated meshes. Figs. 4 and 5 illustrate the average liquid fraction of PCM in relation to the element size and time step for all mesh types. It is clear from these results that in all mesh types, the optimum equivalent size of the elements is approximately 1 mm. Moreover, a time step of 0.1 s is suitable to resolve the transient behavior of the current study.

Comparing numerical results with experimental data is one of the most important steps of numerical analysis. To assess the accuracy of the enthalpy-porosity method used for simulating the PCM melting process, all numerical results are validated against experimental data from Ref. [54] in Fig. 6 . In their research, a PCM called n-eicosane was employed within a cylindrical casing. To validate the PCM simulations in this section, the mesh with hexahedral elements is considered; however, the accuracy of all mesh types is compared in detail in the results and discussion section. From the comparison of results in Fig. 6, it can be found that there is an excellent match between numerical and experimental results, with an error of less than 5 %. As a result, the numerical models and methods which are used in this study are well-suited for analyzing the performance of PCM-based BTMS.

In this section, we discuss a comprehensive validation strategy carried out for the 18650 NMC 811 cell. To begin, the first validation objective is to test the cell under the charge and discharge processes at various C-rates to verify its electrochemical voltage response throughout cell operation. After confirming its ability to predict voltage dynamics correctly, our second validation strategy involves testing the implementation of a conjugate heat transfer model with electrothermal response, starting at an initial ambient temperature of 23 °C.

As discussed previously; to verify the voltage response of the cell, a set of experimental data is used from the Sony NMC 811 VTC4 data sheet [58], in which the cell is tested under an isothermal temperature of 23 °C with varying C-rate. To simulate this test configuration, an isothermal domain is established at 23 °C, with a C-rate ranging from 1C to 15C, using a specified constant current constant voltage (CCCV) charge and discharge protocol.

Fig. 7 (a) summarizes the results of charge validation for a 1C rate. Fig. 7 (b) summarizes validation results for a discharge rate of 1C, 10C, and 15C. These figures confirm the reliability of the electrochemical model by effectively replicating the voltage response under charge and discharge, with an error of less than 6 %.

Fig. 8 provides additional evidence of the accurate implementation of the energy equation coupled with the electrochemical response from the cell by satisfactorily replicating the battery temperature evolution at 5C and 10C discharge rates in comparison to experimental results [58]. The maximum error observed between the numerical and experimental data is 7 %, occurring at the end of the discharge process, while the error remains less than 2 % during the initial stages of discharge. This overall agreement validates the accuracy of the employed simulation model. In this figure, the battery temperature refers to the surface temperature of battery that is measured at the midpoint of the cell length.

The liquid fraction of pure PCM within a cylindrical enclosure is analyzed using three mesh types: hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . Generating a high-quality hexahedral element is a challenging process in engineering applications [66]. The block technique has been used to generate the hexahedral mesh, which involves multiple steps such as creating a block, splitting, blocking associations, etc. Generally, the hexahedral mesh can provide accurate results, but it is not suitable for complex geometries as the geometry may have many small gaps and non-linear curves. Therefore, the tetrahedral mesh is used for complex geometries. On the other hand, the polyhedral mesh can have close accuracy to that of the hexahedral mesh; however, it has a lower number of elements in comparison with hexahedral and tetrahedral meshes [67]. Hence, to investigate the accuracy of PCM melting and computational cost during simulations under different types of mesh, the following investigations have been conducted in the current study.

Table 7 compares the mesh quality metrics and computational cost for the PCM melting simulations within a cylindrical enclosure. In CFD simulations, mesh quality metrics such as maximum aspect ratio and minimum orthogonal quality play a crucial role in ensuring numerical accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency [68]. Generally, the aspect ratio represents the ratio of the longest to the shortest dimension of a cell; a value closer to 1 is preferable, as excessively high aspect ratios can lead to convergence issues. Orthogonal quality measures how well the shape of a cell aligns with ideal geometric properties, with values ranging from 0 (poor quality) to 1 (excellent quality). The higher values of orthogonal quality indicate better numerical accuracy and stability in numerical simulation. Based on the data provided in Table 7, the hexahedral mesh exhibits the lowest aspect ratio (4.4) and the highest orthogonal quality (0.7), indicating superior mesh regularity and alignment. In contrast, tetrahedral and polyhedral meshes have higher aspect ratios (9.9 and 8.5, respectively) and lower orthogonal quality (0.4 for both).

It is evident from the table that the polyhedral mesh offers the lowest computational cost, with only 62 hours and 455,311 iterations required to reach the melting state at 1,000 s. In contrast, the hexahedral and tetrahedral meshes demand significantly more computational resources. The hexahedral mesh, despite having slightly more elements than the tetrahedral mesh, completed the simulation in 143 hours with 479,203 iterations, which is far more efficient than the tetrahedral mesh. The tetrahedral mesh consumed a total of 444 hours and required over 2.7 million iterations. This demonstrates that while the polyhedral mesh uses fewer elements, it offers a clear advantage in terms of both time and iteration count, suggesting that it is the most efficient mesh type for simulating the PCM melting process in a cylindrical enclosure. In comparison, the tetrahedral mesh, despite having fewer elements than the hexahedral mesh, proves to be the least computationally efficient, likely due to the complexity of its element shape, leading to a greater number of iterations and longer time duration.

Based on the above results, the poor performance of the tetrahedral mesh can be attributed to the mesh quality, excessive computational cost, and numerical diffusion. Despite having fewer elements than the hexahedral mesh, the tetrahedral mesh requires a significantly higher number of iterations and longer simulation time. This inefficiency arises from the high aspect ratio and poor orthogonal quality of the tetrahedral elements, which result in a higher number of iterations. The same outcomes have been reported in Ref. [69]. These issues not only degrade the accuracy of the numerical solution but also force the solver to take more iterations to maintain stability and achieve convergence. Furthermore, the higher numerical diffusion associated with the tetrahedral mesh exacerbates this issue, causing less accurate heat transfer predictions and slower convergence. In contrast, the polyhedral mesh reduces numerical diffusion by facilitating mass exchange across multiple faces, enabling more precise solutions with fewer elements [70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the accuracy of the simulation results
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the simulation and experimental comparison for PCM liquid fraction. The figure shows that both the hexahedral and polyhedral meshes yield numerical results that have a high degree of similarity with the experimental results, particularly in the mid to late stages of the melting process. The maximum error observed between the numerical and experimental results occurs with the tetrahedral mesh, showing a deviation of approximately 19 %, while the hexahedral and polyhedral meshes exhibit lower maximum errors of 8.5 % and 12 %, respectively. The hexahedral mesh exhibits the highest consistency, with minimal deviation from the experimental data. The polyhedral mesh also aligns well, although slight deviations can be observed at certain points. On the other hand, the tetrahedral mesh shows the greatest divergence, consistently overestimating the liquid fraction beyond 1,500 s. This indicates that while the tetrahedral mesh may offer more flexibility in simulations with complex geometries, it is less accurate at capturing the phase change behavior compared to the hexahedral and polyhedral meshes.

In this section, the accuracy and computational cost of three different mesh types are investigated for PCM-based BTMS. The generated mesh for the BTMS, composed of hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral elements, is shown in Fig. 11 . In the subsequent sub-sections, a detailed analysis of accuracy and computational cost of the BTMS using three different mesh types is also provided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of BTMS simulation computational cost
ce:section.ce:para: Table 8 compares the mesh quality metrics and computational cost for simulating heat transfer and PCM melting within a PCM-based BTMS (see Fig. 11). The findings reveal that the hexahedral mesh has the lowest aspect ratio and highest orthogonal quality compared to the other mesh types. Furthermore, the polyhedral mesh exhibits the most efficient performance in terms of computational cost, requiring only 42 hours and involving 404,383 iterations to complete the simulation. In contrast, the hexahedral mesh demands a significantly higher computational investment of 56 hours and 400,510 iterations. While this is still relatively efficient, this illustrates a trade-off between mesh complexity and computational time. Notably, the tetrahedral mesh shows the highest computational cost, taking 120 hours and requiring 1,543,835 iterations, which highlights its inefficiency in this specific application. A possible explanation behind the low computational cost of BTMS simulation conducted with polyhedral mesh elements is that the numerical diffusion is reduced because of mass exchange across many faces. This results in a more precise solution obtained with fewer elements [70]. The substantial differences in simulation times and iteration counts suggest that the choice of mesh type can profoundly impact the required computational resources and efficiency when modeling heat transfer and phase change in BTMSs. Therefore, the findings emphasize the importance of selecting an appropriate mesh type to optimize performance while ensuring accuracy in simulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the accuracy of the BTMS simulation results
ce:section.ce:para: Maximum battery temperature is one of the important factors in analyzing the performance of BTMSs, which needs to be kept within the optimum temperature range [71]. Moreover, analyzing the behavior of the PCM liquid fraction within the PCM-based BTMS is essential for determining the effectiveness of system. Hence, comparing these two important parameters for various types of mesh elements in a PCM-based BTMS is essential to investigate the accuracy of numerical results. Fig. 12 illustrates the maximum battery temperature and average PCM liquid fraction versus time. The results shown are for a 3C discharge rate for a battery cell. As can be seen in Fig. 12 (a), initially, a rapid increase in temperature occurs within the first 200 s, followed by a gradual rise that stabilizes after 800 s. This trend arises due to the PCM melting initiation.

In the initial phase of the discharge process, the PCM is in its solid state, and heat dissipation primarily depends on conduction and the sensible heat capacity of the material. As the battery temperature rises and approaches the melting point of PCM, approximately between 36 °C and 38 °C; the phase transition begins. The phase transition becomes noticeable after 200 s in Fig. 12. During this stage, the PCM near the battery surface starts to melt, absorbing a significant portion of the heat generated by the battery. This phase change process stores energy in the form of latent heat, which results in a mitigation of the battery temperature rise rate. In addition, the maximum battery temperature exceeds the optimum range of battery cell (40 °C), which shows that more efficient passive techniques are required to cool down the LIB cell.

In terms of the maximum battery temperature, as presented in Fig. 12 (a), the results from all three mesh types follow a similar trend throughout the simulation period. The temperature differences between the meshes remain minimal, with only slight variations noticeable in the later stages of the simulation. This indicates that despite the different mesh types, the BTMS simulations achieve nearly identical thermal performance, with the polyhedral and hexahedral meshes showing a marginally closer alignment compared to the tetrahedral mesh. However, the overall trend confirms that the mesh type has an insignificant impact on maximum battery temperature.

The liquid fraction results displayed in Fig. 12 (b) indicate a consistent trend for all three mesh types. This figure also shows that the initiation of PCM melting occurs at a similar time for all mesh types, which is approximately 200 s. The liquid fraction of PCM increases gradually as time progresses due to heat absorption from the LIB, with all three meshes producing indistinguishable results. There are no significant discrepancies in the liquid fraction curves, and all meshes follow the same trajectory, especially in the later stages of the simulation. This strong alignment of results suggests that the mesh type has negligible influence on the accuracy of the liquid fraction calculation within the BTMS.

Overall, both battery temperature and PCM liquid fraction results demonstrate that the hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral meshes provide similar outcomes, indicating that any of these mesh types can be used reliably in BTMS simulations without a considerable impact on the final thermal performance results. However, attention should be paid to the computational cost. Considering the results from the previous section, it can be concluded that the BTMS simulations using hexahedral and polyhedral meshes are more suitable due to the shorter computational time required to execute the CFD calculations.

The temperature (in Celsius) and PCM liquid fraction contours for three mesh types are presented in Fig. 13 . Overall, the gradient patterns in both temperature and liquid fraction appear consistent for the hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral meshes, indicating similar thermal and PCM melting behavior. However, minor deviations are observed in the tetrahedral and polyhedral mesh contours, particularly in the radial direction of the battery, where slight irregularities or mutations are visible. These irregularities may arise from the less structured nature of the tetrahedral and polyhedral elements, which can locally impact numerical calculation.

In contrast, the hexahedral mesh exhibits a smoother contour distribution with no noticeable mutations, highlighting its capability to maintain a more uniform temperature and PCM melting profile in CFD simulations of PCM-based BTMSs. This suggests that the hexahedral mesh provides a more reliable and accurate representation of the PCM melting layers, making it a suitable choice for capturing phase change processes with higher precision in BTMS simulations. The results reinforce the conclusion that the hexahedral mesh has the ability to deliver more consistent performance in terms of both temperature and phase change accuracy compared to the other mesh types.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on mesh suitability for complex geometries
ce:section.ce:para: The suitability of the mesh type for accurately capturing the thermal behavior and PCM melting process is also highly dependent on the complexity of the geometry. Among the three mesh types investigated, the hexahedral mesh is the most accurate for simulating these processes; however, its quality and element aspect ratio rely heavily on the simplicity of the geometry. For simple and structured geometries, creating a structured hexahedral mesh is straightforward, but for more complex geometries, this process becomes time-consuming and, in some cases, impractical.

In contrast, the polyhedral mesh offers a strong balance between computational efficiency and accuracy. Although minor uncertainties may arise in post-processing results, the polyhedral mesh significantly reduces the total number of cells and iterations required. This leads to faster simulation times, making it a practical choice for large-scale and complex BTMS applications where minimizing computational cost is crucial.

The tetrahedral mesh, while inherently more adaptable to irregular and intricate geometries, suffers from the highest computational cost. It demands a larger number of cells and iterations, leading to prolonged simulation times. Therefore, for complex geometries, the polyhedral mesh emerges as a preferable alternative to the tetrahedral mesh, offering a reasonable trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.

Based on the previous discussions, it is evident that the tetrahedral mesh is not suitable for PCM-based BTMS simulation due to its low accuracy and high computational cost. Moreover, the PCM liquid fraction contour obtained from the polyhedral mesh showed minor deviations in the results. Hence, a hybrid mesh configuration is proposed to enhance both simulation accuracy and computational efficiency, as illustrated in Fig. 14 . As shown in this figure, polyhedral elements are selected for the LIB and enclosure zones to reduce computation time, whereas hexahedral elements are employed for the PCM zone due to their high accuracy in modeling the phase change behavior of PCM. Mesh quality metrics and computational cost associated with the hybrid mesh are also presented in Fig. 14 for a full discharge process at a 3C rate. In comparison with Table 8, the mesh quality metrics, time duration, and number of iterations for the hybrid mesh are nearly identical to those of the polyhedral mesh. Additionally, the number of elements in the hybrid mesh is about half that of the hexahedral mesh, demonstrating its effectiveness in simulating a PCM-based BTMS. This finding highlights that the proposed hybrid mesh offers good performance in terms of computational cost.

Fig. 15 compares the maximum battery temperature and liquid fraction values obtained from numerical simulations using the hexahedral, polyhedral, and hybrid meshes. A key observation from this figure is the similar temperature variation between the PCM-based BTMS with the hybrid and hexahedral meshes, particularly after 400 s. This trend is expected due to the use of hexahedral elements in the PCM zone. On the other hand, the liquid fraction results of the hybrid mesh exhibit a similar pattern to those of the polyhedral mesh, with a slight deviation from the hexahedral mesh results. However, this deviation diminishes after 800 s, reinforcing the accuracy of the hybrid mesh in modeling the phase change behavior of PCM in BTMS. Overall, these findings indicate that the proposed hybrid mesh is a suitable configuration, providing an efficient balance between accuracy and computational cost in PCM-based BTMS simulations.

This study investigated the performance of three different mesh types: hexahedral, tetrahedral, and polyhedral, in simulating the phase change material (PCM) melting process in a battery thermal management system (BTMS) using computational fluid dynamics (CFD). An electrochemical-thermal model is used to model the heat generation inside the lithium-ion battery. Numerical results indicate that the hexahedral mesh is the most suitable mesh for accurately capturing thermal behavior and the PCM melting process. It ensures a uniform temperature distribution and the most reliable phase change contours, making it highly effective for precise PCM-based BTMS simulations. The polyhedral mesh, while exhibiting some minor uncertainties in post-processing accuracy, stands out for its computational efficiency. It significantly reduces the number of cells and iterations required, resulting in faster simulations without compromising too much on accuracy. This makes it a strong candidate for simulations where computational cost is a key factor, particularly in large-scale applications such as battery pack BTMS and systems with complex geometries. On the other hand, the tetrahedral mesh, despite its suitability for complex geometries, proves to be inefficient for PCM-based BTMS. It has the highest computational cost, requiring the largest number of cells and iterations. Additionally, irregularities observed in the temperature and PCM liquid fraction contours suggest that tetrahedral mesh is not an ideal choice for this type of simulation due to its impact on the precision of results. To address the limitations of individual mesh types, a hybrid mesh configuration combining hexahedral and polyhedral elements has been proposed to improve both accuracy and computational efficiency. The findings confirm that the hybrid mesh successfully balances these factors, making it a promising approach for future PCM-based BTMS simulations. Given these findings, future work should explore the potential of applying similar mesh performance evaluations to other BTMS configurations, such as liquid cooling and air cooling systems, to assess the consistency of mesh-dependent accuracy and computational efficiency across different cooling approaches.
